title: What is a function
instruction:
Think about a library: you ask the librarian for a book and they bring it. You don't need to know how they find it.

A function is the same idea — you give code a name, then just call that name and everything inside runs.

Run this. The `sayHello` function is defined above main and called three times from inside it.
successMessage: Three calls, same output. A function is a reusable recipe.

title: Parameters — make it flexible
instruction:
A function with no parameters always does the same thing. A function with a parameter can adapt.

`greet(name string)` takes a name and prints it. Call it with your own name instead of "Alex".

codeChecks.message: Change the name from "Alex" to your own name.

successMessage: Parameters let you pass information into a function. Same function, different input, different result.

title: Multiple parameters and return values 🤯
instruction:
Functions can take multiple parameters and return values.

`countBooks(shelves, booksPerShelf int) int` takes two ints and returns one. `findBook(category string, index int) (int, int)` returns two ints — Go's way of giving multiple results at once.

Call both functions and print the results.

codeChecks.message: Call countBooks with 3 shelves and 10 books per shelf.
Call findBook with "fiction" and 2.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Two parameters input, two return values output. Go functions are explicit about what they take and give back.

title: Named return values
instruction:
Go lets you name the return values in the function signature. When you do, a bare `return` returns them automatically.

Fill in the body of `bookInfo` so it returns the title and year using named returns.
hint:
Inside bookInfo, just set title and year then write return:
	title = "Dune"
	year = 1965
	return

codeChecks.message: Set title = "Dune"
Set year = 1965
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Named returns make the function self-documenting. The return type tells you what you get back.

title: Variadic functions (any number of args)
instruction:
Sometimes you don't know how many arguments you'll get. `...int` means "zero or more ints."

`totalPages(...)` already sums page counts. Uncomment the second call to prove it works with any number of books.
hint: Remove the // before the second line to uncomment it.
successMessage: One function, any number of arguments. Variadic params handle the rest.

title: Pass a slice to a variadic function
instruction:
What if you already have a slice of pages? Use `pages...` to expand the slice into individual arguments.

Finish the code: create a `pageCounts` slice, then pass it to `totalPages` using the spread operator.

codeChecks.message: Pass the slice to totalPages using slice... syntax.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: The spread operator (...) unpacks a slice into individual arguments. Slice meets variadic.

title: Func as a value 🤯
instruction:
In Go, functions are values. You can store a function in a variable and call it later.

Assign a function that takes a title and returns its length to the `getLength` variable, then call it.

codeChecks.message: Assign a function to getLength using getLength = func...
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Functions as values — assign them, pass them, return them. Go treats functions like any other type.

title: Anonymous function (closure)
instruction:
You can define and use a function inline without naming it. This is called an anonymous function.

Call `fmt.Println` with an anonymous function `func(s string) int { return len(s) }("Dune")`.

codeChecks.message: Use func(s string) int { return len(s) }("Dune")
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Anonymous functions: define, use, forget. Perfect for one-off logic.

title: Pass a function as an argument
instruction:
You can pass one function into another. `applyToBooks` takes a function that transforms a string.

Write a function that returns the number of characters in a title, and pass it to `applyToBooks`.
hint:
countFunc := func(title string) int { return len(title) }
fmt.Println(applyToBooks(countFunc))

codeChecks.message: Pass your function to applyToBooks.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Functions accepting functions — the foundation of callbacks, middleware, and functional patterns in Go.

title: Recursion — a function that calls itself
instruction:
Some problems are best solved by a function that calls itself. This is recursion.

`countdown` prints n, then calls itself with n-1. It stops when n reaches 0.

Fill in the recursive call: `countdown(n - 1)`.

codeChecks.message: Call countdown(n - 1) to make it recursive.
Replace the TODO comment with the recursive call.

successMessage: A function calling itself — recursion. Elegant when used right.

title: Build your own library function
instruction:
Put it all together. Write a function `catalogBook(title string, pages int) string` that returns a formatted string: "Title: Dune (200 pages)".

Then call it with "Dune" and 200 and print the result.

codeChecks.pattern: func catalogBook\(title string, pages int\) string
codeChecks.message: Define catalogBook with the right signature.
Use fmt.Sprintf to format the string.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: You just built a real library function. Parameters, return value, formatting — all working together.
